stories.title: CIM Group, Hulic Acquire West Hollywood Apartment Property
stories.excerpt: CIM Group completed the acquisition of Domain WeHo, a 166-unit apartment community in West Hollywood, in partnership with Hulic Co., Ltd. Although terms were not disclosed, LA Business First reported that the previous owner, RREEF Property Trust, paid more than $103 million in 2019. Domain is a seven-story building with 9,185 square feet of ground-floor retail located at 7141 Santa Monica Blvd. west of La Brea Avenue, a major business corridor and active neighborhood with several restaurants, retail and entertainment venues as well as creative offices. Opened in fall 2017, Domain offers studio, one- and two-bedroom residences, including 33 designated as affordable. According to LA Business First, Domain WeHo sits kitty-corner to the studio campus The Lot at Formosa, also owned by CIM Group. Last year, the firm transferred ownership of the property to a shell company controlled by CIM as part of a recapitalization. The conversations behind tomorrow’s apartment deals happen at Connect Apartments on September 22 in LA. Connect with the capital sources, owners, developers, and brokers driving multifamily investment. Learn more and reserve your seat at www.connectapts2026.com. The post CIM Group, Hulic Acquire West Hollywood Apartment Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CIM Group + Hulic acquire Domain WeHo, 166-unit apartment at $103M+ (2019 basis)
West Hollywood location: prime corridor near retail, entertainment, creative offices
Property includes 33 affordable units; CIM already owns adjacent Lot at Formosa studio campus
stories.summary: CIM Group and Hulic completed acquisition of Domain WeHo, a 166-unit apartment community in West Hollywood, previously valued at $103M+ in 2019. The seven-story building sits on Santa Monica Boulevard west of La Brea, a major business and entertainment corridor, and includes ground-floor retail and 33 affordable units. CIM's adjacent ownership of The Lot studio campus suggests a strategy to control and optimize the creative-office and residential ecosystem in this submarket.
stories.impact: West Hollywood's status as a creative hub and entertainment destination makes it a strong multifamily play for institutional players; CIM's portfolio consolidation signals confidence in sustained demand from entertainment workers and young professionals, supporting higher rents and lower vacancy in the submarket.

stories.title: Retail Veterans Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy Join JLL
stories.excerpt: There are some new recruits at JLL, the brokerage firm announced Monday. Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy, who co-founded retail and entertainment real estate firm Dreamscape, have joined JLL as executive managing director and senior vice president, respectively, to help further drive the firm’s experiential retail, entertainment and mixed-use leasing initiatives. “Josh and Scott have built exceptional careers helping owners and brands create compelling destinations that resonate with today’s consumers,” Patrick A. Smith, vice chairman at JLL, said in a statement. “Their relationships, creativity and deep understanding of the retail landscape will be a tremendous asset to our clients and further enhance the capabilities of both our New York and national project leasing platforms.” The pair have advised on some of the most notable mixed-use and entertainment leases in the country over the last 10 years, including deals at the South Street Seaport in New York City, retail marketplace the Arcade in Nashville, and shopping center Bayside Marketplace in Miami. “Scott and I are excited to join JLL at a time when retail continues to evolve across both urban and suburban markets,” Strauss said in a statement. “While experiential retail has become an increasingly important driver of consumer demand, we remain deeply focused on helping landlords and tenants navigate every aspect of the retail landscape.” Before joining Dreamscape, Strauss and Zinovoy also worked together at independent real estate firm RKF and Newmark, which acquired RKF in 2018. “We were drawn to JLL because of its industry-leading platform, global reach, outstanding reputation and, most importantly, its people and collaborative culture,” Zinovoy said in a statement. “JLL provides the ideal environment to continue growing our business while helping clients bring transformative visions to life.” Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: JLL hires retail veterans Joshua Strauss, Scott Zinovoy from Dreamscape
Duo focused on experiential retail, entertainment, mixed-use leasing nationally
Track record includes South Street Seaport NYC, The Arcade Nashville, Bayside Miami
stories.summary: JLL announced the hire of Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy, co-founders of Dreamscape, as executive managing director and SVP to lead experiential retail and mixed-use leasing. The pair bring a decade of work on major entertainment and destination retail projects across multiple metros. Their appointment signals JLL's push to strengthen capabilities in ground-floor retail activation for mixed-use developments.
stories.impact: Little direct relevance to multifamily core operations, but signals increased capital and broker focus on mixed-use developments with strong ground-floor retail components—a key amenity for Class A multifamily in urban core markets. Developers seeking JLL placement should expect heightened retailer scrutiny and activation expectations.

stories.title: Infinium Wall Systems Takes 30K SF of Office, Retail Space at ESRT’s 1359 Broadway
stories.excerpt: If these walls could talk, they’d talk about all the new leasing in Midtown. Infinium Wall Systems, a company that designs, engineers and installs wall systems and doors for offices, has inked a new office and retail lease spanning 30,017 square feet at Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT)’s 1359 Broadway, the landlord announced Monday. “We are pleased to welcome Infinium to 1359 Broadway,” Ryan Kass, executive vice president, co-head of real estate and chief revenue officer at ESRT, said in a statement. “Innovative companies continue to seek well-located, modernized office space in New York City.” The asking rent and the length of the lease were not disclosed. The average asking rent for office space in Midtown was $84.99 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to Colliers research. Sarah Pontius, Jerica Lam, Carleigh Bettiol and Charlotte Reaman from Artisan Alliance represented Infinium in the deal. Jordan Berger, Shanae Ursini, and Kerry Lavelle represented ESRT in-house, along with Robert Lowe, Ron Lo Russo, Anthony LoPresti and Dan Organ from Cushman & Wakefield. Spokespeople for the brokerages did not immediately respond to requests for comment. “We evaluated numerous locations throughout Midtown, and 1359 Broadway consistently stood out,” Shawn Gaffney, founder of Infinium Wall Systems, told Commercial Observer. “Beyond the quality of the building itself, ESRT’s commitment to sustainability, long-term ownership, and partnership aligned closely with our own values. The southern exposure, private entrance, outdoor terrace, and opportunity to create a truly differentiated space made it the clear choice for our New York flagship.” This deal represents an expansion for Infinium, which also has an office and showroom address at 44 East 32nd Street. Built in 1924, 1359 Broadway is a 22-story office property with other tenants such as artificial intelligence company Scaled Cognition and nonprofit Braven. Amanda Schi
bullets: Infinium Wall Systems leases 30K SF office + retail at ESRT's 1359 Broadway, Midtown NYC
Midtown office asking rent: $84.99/SF in Q2 2026; modern space in high demand
Reflects office market resilience and employer demand for Class A space in CBD
stories.summary: Infinium Wall Systems signed a 30,017-square-foot office and retail lease at Empire State Realty Trust's 1359 Broadway in Midtown Manhattan. The deal reflects ongoing demand for modernized office space in NYC's CBD, with midtown asking rents at $84.99/SF in Q2 2026. The transaction underscores ESRT's success leasing renovated trophy assets.
stories.impact: Marginal relevance to multifamily development. However, sustained office leasing in Midtown suggests continued downtown job density and worker population, supporting baseline rental demand in residential towers and preventing large-scale office-to-residential conversions that would cannibalize apartment supply and rents.

stories.title: Resource Realty Negotiates Warehouse Lease for Amazing Savings
stories.excerpt: Resource Realty of Northern New Jersey (RRNNJ) negotiated a 173,475-square-foot, long-term industrial lease at the newly constructed Spring Brook Commerce Center, located at 128 Iron Mountain Rd. in Mine Hill, NJ, on behalf of closeout retailer Amazing Savings. The transaction was led by the RRNNJ executive brokerage team of principals Tom Consiglio and Scott Peck, who represented Amazing Savings. The landlord is Brookfield Properties, represented by Lee & Associates’ Drew Maffey along with Rick Marchisio, Will Ziegler, Joseph Komitor and Matt Esposito. For more than 20 years, RRNNJ has served as the exclusive real estate advisor to Amazing Savings, navigating the retailer’s regional footprint expansion through multiple market cycles. Under the current leadership of Amazing Savings President Sam Friedland, the relationship continues to thrive. “Working with Tom Consiglio and the Resource Realty NNJ team is always an exceptional experience,” said Friedland. “Our relationship spans many years, and they have delivered for us once again. Tom’s deep market expertise shines through in every transaction.” The post Resource Realty Negotiates Warehouse Lease for Amazing Savings appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Amazing Savings leases 173K SF warehouse at Spring Brook Commerce Center, Mine Hill NJ
Brookfield Properties landlord; long-term retail logistics lease
Reflects retailer demand for modern industrial/logistics real estate in Northern NJ
stories.summary: Resource Realty brokered a 173,475-square-foot industrial lease for closeout retailer Amazing Savings at newly constructed Spring Brook Commerce Center in Mine Hill, NJ. The transaction was with Brookfield Properties and represents a long-term commitment to warehouse/distribution space in Northern New Jersey.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. Industrial leasing strength in NJ suggests continued logistics infrastructure and e-commerce activity, which can drive population and employment growth in surrounding areas, but this deal is too far removed from residential markets to meaningfully affect apartment demand or supply.

stories.title: Prologis Promotes Travis Harvey to Global Head of Development
stories.excerpt: Prologis has promoted Travis Harvey to lead the firm’s global development and construction platform across the Americas, Asia and Europe, Commercial Observer has learned. As global head of development, Harvey will use his more than 20 years of experience in the industry — including 11 years at Prologis — to strengthen the industrial giant’s platform. He will replace Greg Bauer, who recently retired from Prologis after 30 years with the company. “I’m grateful for the opportunity to build on the legacy Greg helped shape. Development is central to how we serve our customers, support the communities where we build and deliver value to investors,” Harvey said in a statement. “My focus will be on strengthening our platform, deepening our use of technology and data, and continuing to support the talented teams that bring our projects to life around the world.” Before joining Prologis, Harvey was vice president of land development at Flagler Global Logistics, where he worked on developing “strategic land assets” in Florida, according to Prologis. Replacing Harvey in his old position as senior vice president and head of development management for Prologis’ U.S. East region is Sheila Sutton, who was previously the firm’s vice president and development officer for the New York and New Jersey markets. Sheila Sutton. PHOTO: Courtesy Prologis Sutton joined Prologis in 2022 from Duke Realty, where she was vice president of construction operations and managed multiple projects throughout New Jersey and Pennsylvania. Prologis acquired Duke Realty in October 2022. “Prologis has established a track record in the East Region for delivering innovative, high-quality logistics projects in some of the most important hubs in the global supply chain, from Toronto down to Miami,” Sutton said in a statement. “I’m excited to build on that momentum with our teams, customers and local partners as we continue to deliver projects that support local economies and communities.” Both Harvey and Sutton
bullets: Prologis promotes Travis Harvey to global head of development across Americas, Asia, Europe
Replaces Greg Bauer after 30-year tenure; Harvey has 11 years at Prologis
Focus: strengthen development platform, expand technology and data use globally
stories.summary: Prologis promoted Travis Harvey, who brings 11 years of experience at the firm, to global head of development and construction, replacing retiring Greg Bauer. Harvey's mandate includes strengthening the development platform, deepening technology and data capabilities, and supporting international project delivery across three continents.
stories.impact: No direct impact on multifamily. Prologis is an industrial REIT; this executive change signals operational focus and capital allocation within industrial/logistics—a separate asset class. However, industrial buildout can indirectly support job growth and population migration to logistics hubs, eventually benefiting multifamily demand in adjacent submarkets.

stories.title: Ryan Demadis Joins IPA Capital Markets in Boston
stories.excerpt: IPA Capital Markets, a division of Marcus & Millichap specializing in capital markets services for major private and institutional clients, has hired Ryan Demadis as a managing director in its Boston office. Demadis will report to Marc Sznajderman, senior managing director and head of production for the firm’s Eastern region. Demadis has financing experience across a wide range of asset classes and has closed more than $1 billion in debt and equity financing. “Ryan brings significant capital markets experience, deep lender relationships and a strong track record advising clients across industrial, multifamily, and other property types,” said Sznajderman. “His expertise in construction financing and structured transactions will be a valuable addition as we continue to expand IPA Capital Markets’ production capabilities throughout the Eastern region.” Before joining IPA Capital Markets, Demadis served as an SVP with Northmarq for more than a decade. Earlier in his career, he was part of the office leasing and investment sales team at RM Bradley in Hartford, CT. The post Ryan Demadis Joins IPA Capital Markets in Boston appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: IPA Capital Markets (Marcus & Millichap) hires Ryan Demadis as managing director, Boston
Demadis: $1B+ in debt/equity closes; expertise in construction financing, multifamily
Strengthens regional capital markets and construction financing capacity in Eastern region
stories.summary: IPA Capital Markets hired Ryan Demadis as managing director in Boston, bringing over $1 billion in closed debt and equity transactions across industrial, multifamily, and mixed-use assets. His background includes deep lender relationships and construction financing expertise from a decade at Northmarq. The hire expands IPA's production capabilities in the Eastern region.
stories.impact: Strengthens capital formation pipeline for multifamily developers in Boston and the Northeast; increased construction financing expertise signals readiness to fund new starts. This hire suggests confidence in deal flow and developer demand for construction debt, supporting an active development market in the region.

stories.title: Infinity, KB Realty Partners Bag 75 Kenmare Street’s Retail Condo for $12M
stories.excerpt: A joint venture went shopping near SoHo and didn’t come away empty-handed. Infinity Real Estate and KB Realty Partners just acquired the retail condominium at 75 Kenmare Street in Manhattan’s Nolita neighborhood—just outside of SoHo — for $11.75 million, Commercial Observer can first report. The transaction, which closed July 16, adds to Infinity’s continued push into SoHo retail, and KB Realty’s expanding portfolio in the neighborhood via both retail and mixed-use investments. 75 Kenmare Street sits in a prime position at the corner of Kenmare and Mulberry streets. Its 10,000-square-foot retail component comprises two commercial units that are fully leased to tenants Undefeated, Nemesis Coffee, Bondi Sushi and M Jewelers. Atop the retail sits a luxury condo component — not included in the purchase — with 38 residential units designed by none other than Lenny Kravitz. First Atlantic Realty was the seller, and was advised by Masonre in the deal. ConnectOne Bank provided Infinity and KB Realty with acquisition financing. “Manhattan retail is where Infinity got its start nearly 20 years ago, and this acquisition marks a deliberate return to that core focus,” Steven Kassin, a managing partner at Infinity Real Estate, said in a statement. “75 Kenmare Street is an attractive asset to continue growing our footprint in Manhattan retail.” Kassin founded Infinity Real Estate in 2005. The firm — which develops, owns and manages real estate across the U.S. — is headquartered in New York, but also has an office in Miami today. “This is exactly the kind of asset we look for,” David Berg, a partner at Infinity Real Estate, said. “Strong in-place tenancy, dependable cash flow from day one, and a tenant mix that genuinely fits the neighborhood.” The deal follows Infinity Real Estate’s acquisition of 40 Bleecker Street in late 2024. The firm paid $13.3 million to Broad Street Development and Crow Holdings for the similarly sized retail condominium, also
bullets: Infinity Real Estate + KB Realty acquire retail condo at 75 Kenmare, Nolita NYC for $11.75M
10K SF retail (2 units, fully leased) + 38-unit luxury condo by Lenny Kravitz above
Prime corner location; Infinity expanding SoHo/Nolita retail footprint
stories.summary: Infinity Real Estate and KB Realty Partners acquired the retail condominium at 75 Kenmare Street in Manhattan's Nolita neighborhood for $11.75 million. The deal includes 10,000 square feet of fully leased ground-floor retail (Undefeated, Nemesis Coffee, Bondi Sushi, M Jewelers) beneath a 38-unit luxury residential tower designed by Lenny Kravitz. The transaction closed in July.
stories.impact: Signals strong retail-anchored mixed-use investment activity in Manhattan's lower Manhattan submarkets; retail condo acquisition at premium cap rate reflects investor confidence in foot traffic, brand cachet, and tourism-driven commerce in Nolita/SoHo. Multifamily developers should note sustained pricing power for premium ground-floor retail components in high-density residential mixed-use.

stories.title: Kiser Group Brokers $12M Sale of Rogers Park Apartment Building
stories.excerpt: Kiser Group Directors Jacob Price and Katie LeGrand facilitated the sale of a 78-unit apartment building located at 6748-50 North Ashland Avenue in Chicago’s Rogers Park neighborhood for $11.5 million. The property was acquired by a local owner-operator with an existing portfolio in the neighborhood. Originally constructed in 1927, the vintage mid-rise building features prominent Art Deco architectural influences. It includes 78 units comprising eight studios, 69 one-bedroom, one-bath apartments, and one two-bedroom, one-bath unit. The property is situated along the Ashland Avenue corridor, near the Morse CTA Red Line station, Rogers Park Metra station, and Lake Michigan, providing convenient access to transit and neighborhood amenities. The transaction closed at 100% of list price, with multiple offers, reflecting strong investor demand for scale across multifamily assets in Chicago’s North Side submarkets. The buyer was motivated by the opportunity to expand their existing footprint in the area, with the property located near their other neighborhood holdings. Stay ahead of the multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Gain firsthand insights from industry leaders on capital markets, development, operations, financing, and investment trends while expanding your network with the people making deals happen. See you on September 22! The post Kiser Group Brokers $12M Sale of Rogers Park Apartment Building appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Kiser Group brokered $11.5M sale of 78-unit Rogers Park apartment building, Chicago
1927 Art Deco vintage mid-rise; location near Morse Red Line, Metra, Lake Michigan
Sold at 100% list price with multiple offers; buyer expanding existing neighborhood portfolio
stories.summary: Kiser Group facilitated the sale of a 78-unit apartment building at 6748-50 North Ashland Avenue in Chicago's Rogers Park for $11.5 million at 100% of list price. The vintage 1927 mid-rise offers strong transit access (Morse CTA Red Line, Rogers Park Metra) and lakefront proximity. The buyer, a local operator, acquired the property to expand their existing footprint in the neighborhood.
stories.impact: Chicago North Side (Rogers Park) multifamily showing strong investor demand for scale acquisition and consolidation; 100% list-price sale with multiple offers signals tight supply and robust rental demand in transit-adjacent, lakefront-proximate submarkets. Signals window for acquisition/development before rents rise further in this infill location.

stories.title: Mindspace to Open Largest U.S. Location at Transamerica Complex
stories.excerpt: Global flexible workspace provider Mindspace has signed a new 54,000-square-foot lease at Two Transamerica Plaza, located at 505 Sansome St., San Francisco, within the Transamerica complex, marking the company’s second location in San Francisco and its largest in the U.S. The new lease nearly triples Mindspace’s local presence and also makes it the largest tenant at Two Transamerica Plaza. The lease was signed with YODA PLC, the Cyprus-based real estate investment and development firm that recently acquired the Transamerica complex, marking one of the company’s first major leasing transactions following the acquisition. “San Francisco remains one of the world’s leading innovation hubs, and we’re seeing growing demand from companies looking for premium workplaces that help attract talent and bring people back together,” said Mark Goldfinger, managing director of Mindspace North America. “Opening our West Coast flagship at the iconic Transamerica complex is a strong vote of confidence in the city’s future.” The post Mindspace to Open Largest U.S. Location at Transamerica Complex appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Mindspace signs 54K SF at Two Transamerica Plaza, San Francisco; largest U.S. location
Lease triples local presence; marks largest tenant at Transamerica complex
YODA PLC (Cyprus investor) recent acquirer; strong first leasing transaction post-acquisition
stories.summary: Flexible workspace provider Mindspace signed a 54,000-square-foot lease at Two Transamerica Plaza (505 Sansome St.) in San Francisco, marking its largest U.S. location and second SF site. The lease nearly triples Mindspace's local footprint and makes it the largest tenant at the property. YODA PLC, a Cyprus-based RE firm that recently acquired the Transamerica complex, completed one of its first major leases post-acquisition.
stories.impact: San Francisco office market shows resurgence in high-profile lease signings and flex-workspace adoption; sustained office demand (even in flex format) reduces near-term conversion pressure on Class A office towers and supports continued downtown employment density. Indirect positive signal for SF multifamily rents in core markets, as downtown employment anchors residential markets.

stories.title: Law Firm Hodgson Russ Renews 20K SF at Fisher Brothers’ 605 Third Avenue
stories.excerpt: Law firm Hodgson Russ spearheaded a rush of new leases at Fisher Brothers’ 605 Third Avenue. The firm renewed its 19,900-square-foot lease on the 23rd floor of the tower, located between East 39th and East 40th streets, in June, according to the landlord. Newmark’s Brian Cohen and Kyle Ciminelli negotiated the 10-year renewal on behalf of the law firm. Several deals inked in the building since April secured rents up to $120 per square foot, according to Fisher Brothers. The landlord, represented in-house by Clark Briffel, did not disclose exact prices. Other recent activity in the 44-story building included private equity firm Coalesce Capital’s 16,900-square-foot renewal and expansion in June. The deal secured Coalesce its home on the 36th floor for the next five years. Meanwhile, investment firm Lionheart Strategic Management also expanded in June, taking 6,400 additional square feet on the 16th floor for 10 years. Lionheart’s total footprint at the property following the new expansion was unclear. Both firms were represented in-house in the lease deals. These busy tenants can expect to remain caffeinated for the next decade, at least, as ground-floor retail tenant Starbucks signed a 10-year renewal across 1,800 square feet in July. David Firestein of TSCG negotiated on behalf of the coffee chain. A 130,000-square-foot block of availability on 605 Third Avenue’s fourth, fifth and sixth floors remains, according to the Fisher Brothers. The landlord has retained a CBRE team of Howard Fiddle, Peter Turchin, Ben Joseph, Brett Shannon, Max Tarter, Zachary Price and Natalie Chung to market the spaces. Spokespeople for Newmark and the tenants did not immediately respond to requests for comment. TSCG could not be reached for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Law firm Hodgson Russ renews 19.9K SF at Fisher Brothers' 605 Third Ave, Midtown NYC
10-year renewal at $120/SF asking; other recent deals in building up to $120/SF
Coalesce Capital, Lionheart Strategic also expanded/renewed in June; strong leasing momentum
stories.summary: Law firm Hodgson Russ renewed its 19,900-square-foot lease on the 23rd floor of Fisher Brothers' 605 Third Avenue in Midtown (between E. 39th–40th), securing a 10-year term. Recent transactions in the 44-story tower achieved rents up to $120/SF. Concurrent leasing activity includes Coalesce Capital's expansion and Lionheart Strategic's expansion, signaling robust demand in Midtown.
stories.impact: Midtown Manhattan office leasing showing strength and pricing power at $120/SF; sustained corporate/professional services occupation suggests continued downtown employment concentration and worker commute patterns supporting multifamily residential demand in Manhattan core and adjacent outer-borough transit hubs.

stories.title: Tannos secures $28M in construction financing for Houston area mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Tannos Development Groups’ Friendswood mixed-use project is one step closer to coming to fruition.  The Friendswood-based developer secured $28 million in mid-construction financing for The Albritton, a mixed-use development being built at 408 South Friendswood Drive, according to a release from Miami-based Concord Summit Capital, which arranged the deal.  Concord Summit’s Daniel J. Eidson and Ben Applebaum sourced the financing. The debt, which has a 70 percent loan-to-value ratio, is supposed to carry the project “through the remainder of construction,” Eidson said in the release. S3 Capital provided the financing, Commercial Observer reported.  The Albritton will feature 111 multifamily units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tannos Development secures $28M construction financing for The Albritton mixed-use, Friendswood TX
Project: 111 units, 11K SF retail, 9K SF office; completion 2026; 70% LTV debt
Part of larger Tannos pipeline: Friendswood City Center ($750M, 106 acres) + Magnolia Town Center ($1B, 200 acres)
stories.summary: Tannos Development secured $28 million in mid-construction financing from S3 Capital (arranged by Concord Summit) for The Albritton, a mixed-use project in Friendswood, TX featuring 111 apartments, 11,000 SF retail, and 9,000 SF office, targeting 2028 completion. The 70% LTV debt carries the project through construction. Friendswood (25 miles SE of Downtown Houston) is the hub of Tannos' expanded portfolio, including the $750M Friendswood City Center and $1B Magnolia Town Center.
stories.impact: Tannos' aggressive capital raise and phased mixed-use buildout in Houston exurbs signals developer confidence in sustained demand from Houston metro's continued outward sprawl; construction financing availability and favorable LTV indicate lender appetite for suburban multifamily-anchored mixed-use. Watch absorption and rents in Friendswood as leading indicator of Houston's housing supply elasticity.

stories.title: Buckling scare puts MetroLoft’s ‘scoop-and-stack’ playbook under microscope
stories.excerpt: The dramatic structural failure at MetroLoft’s conversion of Pfizer’s former Midtown headquarters is drawing attention to one of the developer’s signature redevelopment techniques. The temporary evacuation around 235 East 42nd Street earlier this month, after columns buckled and floors sagged during construction, has brought renewed focus to MetroLoft founder Nathan Berman’s “scoop-and-stack” approach, according to Crain’s. While investigators continue probing what caused the incident, engineers and developers say the method itself underscores the growing complexity of converting postwar office towers into apartments. The strategy essentially hollows out portions of a building’s lower floors, creating interior lightwells while transferring that unused […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: MetroLoft's Pfizer headquarters conversion (235 E 42nd, Midtown NYC) experiences structural buckling during construction
Signature 'scoop-and-stack' method: hollow lower floors, create lightwells, add floors atop via air rights
Project expands from ~973K SF to 1.3M SF; includes 15-story cantilevered addition above failure point
stories.summary: MetroLoft's office-to-residential conversion of Pfizer's former Midtown headquarters (235 E 42nd St.) experienced a structural failure—columns buckled and floors sagged—prompting temporary evacuation. The incident has drawn scrutiny to MetroLoft founder Nathan Berman's signature "scoop-and-stack" technique, which hollows out lower floors to create interior lightwells and transfers development potential to new stories added above. At the Pfizer site, the strategy was designed to expand the project from ~973,000 SF to 1.3M SF, including a 15-story cantilevered addition above the failure location.
stories.impact: Major red flag for office-to-residential conversion feasibility and economics in Midtown Manhattan. Structural and engineering complexity of retrofitting postwar office towers—especially those with oversized floor plates and deep interiors—is higher and riskier than initially underwritten. Developers and lenders may tighten underwriting on similar Midtown conversion deals, slow starts, and increase contingency reserves; cap rates may compress if risk perception rises.

stories.title: Stetson University plans Tampa development as higher ed bets on mixed-use districts
stories.excerpt: Stetson University is joining the higher education trend of investing in mixed-use districts, with the redevelopment of its Tampa Law Center. Stetson is partnering with New York-based Bromley Companies on plans to develop 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37,500 square feet of retail space and 440,000 square feet of office space, along with a new law center, on the 6.5-acre site in the Tampa Heights district, according to ConnectCRE.  Stetson University acquired the property from the City of Tampa in 2002, according to the Tampa Business Journal. It was previously the site of the Tampa Heights police station.  The university’s […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Stetson University + Bromley Companies redevelop Tampa Law Center site (6.5 acres, Tampa Heights)
Plans: 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37.5K SF retail, 440K SF office + new law center
Part of national higher-ed trend monetizing real estate; USF Fletcher District ($268M) nearby under construction
stories.summary: Stetson University is partnering with New York-based Bromley Companies to redevelop its Tampa Law Center into a mixed-use district on 6.5 acres in Tampa Heights. The project includes 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37,500 SF retail, and 440,000 SF office, plus a new law school. Construction starts late 2025, contingent on Tampa City Council rezoning approval. The project mirrors a national trend of universities monetizing real estate; USF's $268M Fletcher District is nearby and under construction.
stories.impact: Tampa Heights submarkets will see significant multifamily infill density driven by institutional capital and mixed-use development anchored by education/law school; 670 units at one site signals a major absorption event. Developers should track zoning approval timing and phasing; this institutional player's commitment validates the submarket and may trigger competing development activity. Rents in Tampa Heights should benefit from placemaking investment and student population concentration.

stories.title: Fraud scheme: real estate journalism’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads”
stories.excerpt: Real estate’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads” usually includes some version of the phrase “fraud scheme.” DFW real estate’s latest 15-car pileup on I-35 is the case of two Frisco entrepreneurs who already had a go-round with allegations of a “Ponzi scheme” on their track record before concocting promotional materials for a luxury waterfront development on the San Gabriel River that they never intended to build, the Dallas Morning News reported. Promotional materials for the 400-acre development showed a mixed-use development with office retail, restaurants, parks and a canal big enough for boats on the Central Texas river […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco entrepreneurs Krishnan, Gounder liable for $400M fraud (breach, false development scheme)
Fake mixed-use project on San Gabriel River; swindled $21M infrastructure commitment from Energy Commissioning
Pair previously faced SEC restraining order for alleged $130M Ponzi scheme (2023)
stories.summary: A Williamson County jury found Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder liable for breach of contract and fraud, ordering $400M in damages (including $300M punitive) to Energy Commissioning Inc. for a fake 400-acre mixed-use development on the San Gabriel River that never existed. The defendants swindled the contractor out of $21M in infrastructure costs. This is the pair's second major fraud allegation; the SEC hit them with a restraining order and asset freeze in 2023 over an alleged $130M Ponzi scheme targeting DFW's Indian-American community.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development, but signals need for enhanced due diligence on land development partners and JV sponsors in DFW market, particularly around infrastructure and infrastructure financing. High-profile fraud case may increase lender scrutiny on developer backgrounds and project verification, adding underwriting time and cost for legitimate deals in the region.

stories.title: Align ups unit count, lowers height for controversial Marina Safeway project
stories.excerpt: Align Real Estate is making its contentious Marina Safeway redevelopment a little shorter and a little denser, revising its proposal as it tries to steer one of San Francisco’s most closely watched housing projects through city approval. The San Francisco-based developer submitted updated plans that would reduce the project’s tallest towers from 25 and 22 stories to 22 and 18 stories while increasing the residential count from 790 to 848 units, the San Francisco Chronicle reported. The redesign trims roughly 40 feet from the east tower and 23 feet from the west tower with the entire development at 15 Marina […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Align Real Estate revises Marina Safeway (15 Marina Blvd, SF) mixed-use plan; reduces height, adds units
New plan: 848 units (vs. 790); towers reduced from 25/22 stories to 22/18 stories; 989.5K SF total
Mayor Lurie still opposes; planning review under state AB 2011 (streamlined housing approvals)
stories.summary: Align Real Estate submitted revised plans for its contentious Marina Safeway redevelopment at 15 Marina Boulevard, San Francisco, reducing building heights (25/22 stories to 22/18) while increasing residential units from 790 to 848. The 989,500 SF project optimizes floor plans and shrinks ground-floor non-residential uses to fit more apartments. Despite the revision, Mayor Daniel Lurie remains opposed, though the project continues planning review under AB 2011, California's streamlined housing approval law for qualifying commercial sites.
stories.impact: SF housing supply battle highlights political/regulatory headwinds for large mixed-use development on waterfront sites; even with state-level streamlining (AB 2011), local opposition slows approvals and adds cost. Developers in CA should expect compressed entitlement timelines and design/unit count revisions as a norm, not exception. Marina Safeway's near-waterfront location will be closely monitored as bellwether for SF's willingness to densify; approval would signal supply relief, rejection would strengthen NIMBY precedent.

stories.title: Miami design board swoons over OKO Lilli, 13th Floor’s Nobu condo towers
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s second condo tower in Edgewater and 13th Floor Investments’ proposal for a Nobu-branded residential building next to a historic Brickell church are advancing.  The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of both developments on Wednesday, with most board members gushing over the designs of what would be the latest luxury tower additions to the city’s quickly evolving skyline. One board member voted no on Doronin’s planned building, citing issues with some of the design.  Miami-based OKO Group, led by Doronin, plans the 53-story, 117-unit project, called OKO Lilli Tower, on the half-acre lot at 717 Northeast […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Design Review Board approves OKO Lilli (53-story, 117-unit condo tower, Edgewater) + 619 Brickell by Nobu (75-story, 321-unit tower, Brickell)
OKO led by developer Vlad Doronin; Nobu tower led by 13th Floor (Arnaud Karsenti)
Both Edgewater and Brickell seeing significant high-rise residential development; UDRB approval advisory only
stories.summary: Miami's Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of two luxury residential towers: OKO Lilli (53-story, 117-unit Edgewater condo tower by Vlad Doronin with baywalk and retail) and 619 Brickell by Nobu (75-story, 321-unit tower in Brickell by 13th Floor Investments, featuring private-elevator residences and luxury branding). UDRB approval is advisory; final authority rests with the city's planning director. One board member voted no on OKO Lilli citing design concerns.
stories.impact: Miami's Edgewater and Brickell submarkets experiencing high-velocity luxury condo development; UDRB approval clears design hurdles and signals likely final sign-off, advancing competing projects toward permitting and construction. Developers should monitor absorption rates and pricing power at competing ultra-luxury towers (esp. Nobu-branded); if supply outpaces demand, rents/pricing may soften. Also note public baywalk and mixed-use components—amenity expectations rising for Miami waterfront multifamily.
